flashcards.front: What is tree traversal in a binary tree?
flashcards.back: Tree traversal is the process of visiting each node in a binary tree exactly once in a systematic way to collect node information. It is a fundamental operation used for searching, printing, or processing all nodes.

Preorder traversal visits nodes in the order: Node -> Left Subtree -> Right Subtree. It is used for creating copies of trees, prefix expression evaluation, and getting prefix notation of an expression tree.
Inorder traversal visits nodes in the order: Left Subtree -> Node -> Right Subtree. For Binary Search Trees, this gives nodes in sorted (ascending) order.
Postorder traversal visits nodes in the order: Left Subtree -> Right Subtree -> Node. It is used for deleting trees, evaluating postfix expressions, and getting postfix notation.
Level Order traversal visits nodes level by level from top to bottom, using a queue. It is a breadth-first approach that processes all nodes at current depth before moving to the next level.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Depth-First and Breadth-First traversal?
flashcards.back: Depth-First (preorder, inorder, postorder) explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. Breadth-First (level order) explores all nodes at current depth before moving deeper.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of tree traversal?
flashcards.back: For a binary tree with n nodes, all standard traversals (preorder, inorder, postorder, level order) have O(n) time complexity as each node is visited exactly once.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of recursive tree traversal?
flashcards.back: Recursive tree traversal uses the system stack with space complexity O(h), where h is the height of the tree. In worst case (skewed tree), h = n giving O(n) space.

flashcards.front: Why is a stack used in iterative tree traversal?
flashcards.back: A stack is used to simulate recursion in iterative traversals. It stores nodes to be processed, enabling backtracking to explore right subtrees after processing left subtrees.

flashcards.front: Why is a queue used in level order traversal?
flashcards.back: A queue enables breadth-first processing by maintaining nodes in FIFO order. It ensures nodes are processed level by level, with children enqueued for future processing.

flashcards.front: What property does inorder traversal have in a Binary Search Tree?
flashcards.back: In a BST, inorder traversal always produces nodes in sorted (ascending) order. This property is used to validate if a tree is a BST and for sorted output.

flashcards.front: What is backtracking in tree traversal?
flashcards.back: Backtracking is the process of returning to previously visited nodes to explore unvisited paths. In recursive traversals, it happens automatically when function calls return.

flashcards.front: How can you construct a unique binary tree from preorder and inorder traversals?
flashcards.back: Given preorder and inorder sequences, a unique binary tree can be constructed. Preorder identifies root, inorder splits left and right subtrees. Both sequences together uniquely determine the tree.
Morris Traversal is an inorder traversal technique that uses no recursion and no stack by temporarily modifying the tree structure, creating threaded connections to ancestors.

flashcards.front: What are expression trees and which traversal gives prefix/postfix form?
flashcards.back: Expression trees are binary trees representing arithmetic expressions. Inorder gives infix notation, preorder gives prefix notation (Polish), and postorder gives postfix notation (Reverse Polish).
